summary: Manish Jones was an old man I knew who lived to be 96 years old. He lived during slavery times in Georgia and often told us stories about how slaves managed to survive under harsh conditions. Manish was a strong and brave man who refused to be broken by the many whippings he endured. One time, he escaped the overseers and bloodhounds by cleverly washing his feet in soapy creek water to confuse the dogs' scent. He survived in the woods for months, relying on stolen food and help from other slaves.

Bella was the wife of Manish Jones, the old man who lived through slavery in Georgia. Though the transcript doesn't give many details about her, I know she was part of Manish's life during those difficult times. She was mentioned as his spouse, sharing the hardships and stories of their era. Their relationship was part of the larger story of survival and resilience during slavery.

I grew up hearing stories about slavery in the United States, especially from people like Manish Jones who lived through it in Georgia. During that time, slaves were controlled strictly by landowners and slaveowners, and everything they raised went to the owner's storehouse. Slaves often had to steal food secretly to survive, and the fear of whipping and punishment was constant. The stories of running from overseers and bloodhounds, hiding food like sweet potatoes, and living in the woods show how brutal and oppressive slavery was.

Georgia was the place where Manish Jones and many other slaves lived during the time of slavery in the United States. It was a state where slavery was very strong, and the landowners and slaveowners held all the power. The stories I heard about Manish escaping into the woods, hiding in swamps, and the slaves stealing sweet potatoes all happened in Georgia. It was a harsh environment for slaves, but also a place where they showed great courage and resilience.

Bloodhounds were the dogs used by overseers to track runaway slaves like Manish Jones in Georgia during slavery times. I remember how Manish described escaping their hunt by washing his feet in soapy creek water to confuse the dogs' scent. The bloodhounds would sniff around the woods and swamps, but Manish's clever trick made them lose the trail. These dogs were feared because they were trained to track slaves, but Manish's story shows how he outsmarted them.

Sweet potatoes were a vital food source for slaves during slavery times in Georgia, especially when food was scarce. I heard how Manish Jones and other slaves secretly stole sweet potatoes and hid them in a special cache covered with blankets and dirt to keep them safe from slaveowners. They called it a 'sweet potato hill,' and it was better than a storehouse for them. This secret food supply helped Manish survive while he was hiding in the woods for months.

The oak wood was used by Manish Jones and the slaves to cook food secretly during slavery times. I remember the story about how they cooked hog and sweet potatoes over an oak wood fire, which made hot ash and a low fire to keep the cooking hidden. The oak fire was important because it helped them prepare food without drawing attention from slaveowners. It was part of the secret survival tactics slaves used in Georgia.
